metadata.title: Run a Three-Failure Taxonomy on Two LLM DAGs with Claude
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration: Two different LLMs propose two different DAGs for the same marketing problem. Run the three-failure taxonomy — watch them fail differently.
beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear.

beats.narration: Three ways a DAG can be wrong: wrong variables, wrong arrows, wrong conditioning. One taxonomy, applied to any model's causal proposal.
We give Claude two DAG edge lists for a B2B SaaS email-to-conversion problem and ask it to apply the three-failure taxonomy to each.

props.command:
claude "Here are two DAGs for email → conversion:
  DAG-A: [email, open, click, demo, convert]
  DAG-B: [email, open, intent, demo, convert]
For each: apply three-failure taxonomy:
  (1) wrong variables
  (2) wrong arrows
  (3) wrong conditioning"

beats.narration: Claude returns the taxonomy table. DAG-A: wrong variables — misses intent signal. Wrong arrows — open causes demo directly, skipping click. DAG-B: wrong conditioning — intent is a mediator, conditioning on it blocks the email-to-convert path.

props.code:
# three_failure.md — Claude output

           DAG-A          DAG-B
Wrong var: missing intent  missing click
Wrong arr: open→demo      demo→intent (rev)
Wrong cond: none          conditions on
                          mediator 'intent'

# DAG-B worst failure: conditioning on
# intent blocks the causal path
# email → intent → demo → convert

beats.narration: The taxonomy reveals DAG-B has the more dangerous failure: conditioning on a mediator blocks the causal path we are trying to estimate. DAG-A's wrong arrow is a structural error but does not bias the estimate if we condition correctly.
For the worst-performing DAG — DAG-B — we ask Claude to propose a corrected version and apply the taxonomy again.

props.command:
claude "DAG-B failed on wrong conditioning.
Propose corrected DAG-B.
Apply three-failure taxonomy again.
Verify intent is no longer a mediator."

beats.narration: Corrected DAG-B moves intent upstream as a confounder affecting both email open rate and demo likelihood. The taxonomy now shows no wrong-conditioning failures. Structural errors corrected.

The three-failure taxonomy is a structured audit tool: wrong variables, wrong arrows, wrong conditioning. Applying it to two models on the same task reveals systematic weaknesses in how language models represent causal structure.
Next: draft and stress-test a professional DAG defense — and find the fabricated citation in paragraph two.
Like and subscribe for more causal reasoning with Claude.
